description: Activities table at 1.83B rows is 14% from INTEGER overflow. The migration converts the PK and FK columns from INTEGER to BIGINT. Review the locking, replication, and operational implications.
review_checklist: Quantify rewrite cost of ALTER COLUMN TYPE on a multi-billion row table
Identify staged migration patterns to avoid downtime
Check sequence object type and dependent indexes

ground_truth.description: ALTER COLUMN TYPE on a 1.83B-row table forces a FULL TABLE REWRITE under AccessExclusiveLock. Estimated runtime: 8-15 hours during which the table is completely unavailable. This is the textbook 'never do this in production' migration. Correct pattern: (1) add new column id_bigint as BIGINT, (2) backfill in batches with COPY-style logic, (3) keep both columns in sync with triggers, (4) cut over with constraint swap, (5) drop old column.

Migration does NOT alter the underlying SEQUENCE — `activities_id_seq` is still type integer. After 'fixing' the column, INSERTs continue to fail at ~250M more inserts because nextval() returns integer. Required: ALTER SEQUENCE activities_id_seq AS BIGINT (PG 10+).

ALTER COLUMN TYPE on activity_reactions.activity_id ALSO rewrites the table (estimated 800M rows, ~6 hours) AND must validate FK integrity against the activities rewrite. Sequenced after activities, this means consecutive multi-hour outages. Use staged backfill pattern instead.

Architectural review: activities is the canonical example of a table that should have been BIGINT from day one. Capacity-planning rule: any table whose row count is bounded by user × time should default to BIGINT. INTEGER is appropriate only for bounded enumerations (statuses, types). For all generated IDs use BIGINT.

activity_reactions.id is also SERIAL (INTEGER). This table is even larger writes-per-sec than activities — it will hit overflow before activities does. Same INTEGER → BIGINT migration needed here, surfacing same operational risk.

WAL volume during ALTER TYPE is enormous — full row rewrite logs every row twice (delete + insert). For a 1.83B-row table this can produce 200-400GB of WAL, risking replication slot disk exhaustion and replica out-of-sync. Coordinate with infra team on WAL retention, replica disk space, and consider pausing logical replication slots.
